perturbation.original_text: The Initial Term shall automatically renew for one-month periods thereafter unless either party provides 30- days advance notice of termination, unless earlier terminated pursuant to Section 2.2 hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The Initial Term shall automatically renew for one-month periods thereafter unless either party provides 10- days advance notice of termination, unless earlier terminated pursuant to Section 2.2 hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the advance notice for termination to 10 days may conflict with New York law, which requires reasonable notice depending on the circumstances and the ongoing business relationship.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law requires 'reasonable' notice for termination. While 'reasonable' is subject to interpretation, ten days for a potentially ongoing tech development agreement may be deemed unreasonable, especially if significant resources or dependencies exist. Thirty days is more likely to be considered reasonable.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 30-days' notice for termination, while the changed text reduces this to 10 days. The Law Explanation indicates that New York law requires 'reasonable' notice, and ten days may be deemed unreasonable. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly define 'reasonable notice' or provide a specific timeframe, the Law Explanation's assessment that ten days may be insufficient, especially considering the nature of a tech development agreement, suggests a contradiction. Reducing the notice period potentially undermines the 'reasonable' standard required by New York law.

perturbation.original_text: During the Term of this Agreement and thereafter for a period of five (5) years, the parties shall be bound by all of the protective terms and conditions of the NDA.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term of this Agreement and thereafter for a period of one (1) year, the parties shall be bound by all of the protective terms and conditions of the NDA.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under New York law, particularly given the nature of the technology involved.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law & Rules § 214
perturbation.law_explanation: The statute of limitations for misappropriation of trade secrets in New York is three years. A one-year confidentiality period might be considered unreasonable and insufficient to protect against trade secret violations, especially concerning complex technology, since claims may be brought up to 3 years after the act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year. The Law Explanation states that the statute of limitations for misappropriation of trade secrets in New York is three years. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that a one-year confidentiality period may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially regarding complex technology, because claims may be brought up to 3 years after the act. Therefore, because the contract term is shorter than the statute of limitations, the modified term creates an inconsistency that may lead to legal consequences.

perturbation.original_text: It is hereby irrevocably agreed that legal jurisdiction and venue for any proceeding arising out of this Agreement shall be in the state or federal courts located in the County of Monroe, State of New York, United States.
perturbation.changed_text: It is hereby agreed that legal jurisdiction and venue for any proceeding arising out of this Agreement shall be in the courts located in Hong Kong.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the jurisdiction to Hong Kong courts could conflict with New York law if the agreement is deemed to have significant connections to New York and the choice of law provision designates New York law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under NY CPLR 327, a court may stay or dismiss an action when it finds that in the interest of substantial justice the action should be heard in another forum. Changing the venue to Hong Kong while the company DSS is based in New York could be seen as violating the principle of substantial justice in some cases if the burden of litigating in Hong Kong is too high and if the technology in question is physically located in New York.

Inconvenient forum. (a) When the court finds that in the interest of substantial justice the action should be heard in another forum, the court, on the motion of any party, may stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. The domicile or residence in this state of any party to the action shall not preclude the court from staying or dismissing the action. (b) Notwithstanding the provisions of subdivision (a) of this rule, the court shall not stay or dismiss any action on the ground of inconvenient forum, where the action arises out of or relates to a contract, agreement or undertaking to which section 5-1402 of the general obligations law applies, and the parties to the contract have agreed that the law of this state shall govern their rights or duties in whole or in part.
perturbation.justification: The change in jurisdiction from New York to Hong Kong directly contradicts the principle of 'inconvenient forum' as outlined in NY CPLR 327. While CPLR 327(a) allows a court to stay or dismiss an action if another forum is more appropriate, the Law Explanation suggests the change might violate the principle of substantial justice if litigating in Hong Kong poses a significant burden, given DSS's location in New York. The original text provided certainty by irrevocably agreeing that legal jurisdiction would be in New York. The changed text removes this certainty, thus contradicting New York Law.
